learning_objectives.text: Define key concepts in digital health including telemedicine, mHealth, electronic health records, and health information systems
Identify the core components of health technology assessment frameworks including safety, effectiveness, cost-effectiveness, and ethical dimensions
Explain the principles of appropriate technology and frugal innovation for low-resource settings
Describe the role of artificial intelligence and machine learning in diagnostic applications, predictive analytics, and clinical decision support
Apply implementation science frameworks to design health technology scale-up strategies
Apply health technology assessment methodologies to evaluate digital health interventions
Analyze the factors contributing to digital divides in health and strategies to promote equitable technology access
Analyze the ethical implications of AI/ML applications in healthcare including algorithmic bias, data privacy, and clinical accountability
Design a digital health intervention appropriate for a specific low-resource context incorporating user-centered design principles
Develop a comprehensive strategy for health information system strengthening in resource-limited settings
Evaluate health technologies using established assessment frameworks considering clinical effectiveness, cost-effectiveness, equity, and implementation feasibility
Critically appraise regulatory frameworks for digital health and AI in different jurisdictions

key_terms.definition: The broad umbrella term encompassing eHealth, mHealth, and emerging technologies using computing platforms, connectivity, software, and sensors for healthcare purposes.
The delivery of healthcare services using telecommunications technology when patients and providers are not in the same physical location.
Medical and public health practice supported by mobile devices including mobile phones, patient monitoring devices, personal digital assistants, and wireless devices.
Digital versions of patients' paper charts containing medical history, diagnoses, medications, treatment plans, immunization dates, allergies, radiology images, and laboratory results.
A multidisciplinary process that uses explicit methods to determine the value of a health technology at different points in its lifecycle, informing decision-making.
Technology that is suitable to the social and economic conditions of the geographic area where it is used, meets needs of users, and can be sustained by local resources.
Innovation that seeks to minimize the use of material and financial resources in the complete cycle of design, production, distribution, consumption, and disposal.
Medical diagnostic testing performed at or near the site of patient care, enabling clinical decisions during the patient encounter.
The use of complex algorithms and software to emulate human cognition in analysis, interpretation, and comprehension of medical and healthcare data.
A subset of AI where algorithms improve through experience without being explicitly programmed, learning patterns from data to make predictions or decisions.
An integrated system for collecting, processing, reporting, and using health information and knowledge to influence policy-making, program action, and research.
The ability of different information systems, devices, and applications to access, exchange, integrate, and use data in a coordinated manner.
The gap between demographics and regions that have access to modern information and communications technology and those that have limited access.
The adoption of advanced or state-of-the-art technology in an application area where immediate prior technology has not been adopted.
Software with source code available for modification and redistribution, often developed collaboratively and shared freely.

theoretical_frameworks.application: Understanding user adoption of health technologies through perceived usefulness and perceived ease of use
Analyzing the spread of health technologies across populations and health systems
Guiding implementation of health technology interventions in complex health systems
Evaluating health technology programs across Reach, Effectiveness, Adoption, Implementation, and Maintenance
Situating health technology within health system strengthening efforts
Developing health technologies that meet user needs and contextual requirements
Incorporating equity considerations into evaluation of health technologies

content_sections.title: Introduction to Health Technology and Innovation
key_topics: Defining health technology in global context
Evolution of health technology from traditional to digital
The innovation ecosystem in global health
Technology's role in achieving Universal Health Coverage

content_sections.title: Digital Health: Foundations and Applications
key_topics: Remote patient monitoring and wearable technologies

content_sections.title: Health Information Systems
key_topics: Disease surveillance and early warning systems

content_sections.title: Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning in Health
key_topics: Natural language processing in clinical settings

content_sections.title: Innovation Ecosystems and Technology Transfer
key_topics: Open source approaches to health technology

content_sections.title: Digital Divides and Equity in Health Technology
Implementation and Scale-Up
key_topics: Monitoring and evaluation of technology programs

content_sections.title: Future Directions
key_topics: Preparing health systems for technological change

case_studies.title: Rwanda's National Digital Health Transformation: OpenMRS and RapidSMS
key_themes: Integration of mHealth with community health workers
Political commitment and health system strengthening
key_lessons: Strong political commitment enables rapid scale-up
Open source solutions reduce costs and increase adaptability
Integration with community health programs extends reach
Continuous iteration based on user feedback improves adoption
outcomes: Improved maternal and child health outcomes

case_studies.title: GeneXpert MTB/RIF: Transforming Tuberculosis Diagnosis in Low-Resource Settings
key_lessons: Negotiated pricing expands access in LMICs
Integration into national programs requires infrastructure investment
Ongoing innovation (Xpert Ultra) continues to improve performance
outcomes: Reduced time to TB diagnosis from weeks to hours

case_studies.title: AI-Powered Diabetic Retinopathy Screening: From Algorithm Development to Population-Scale Implementation

geographic_focus.who_region: South-East Asia Region (SEARO), Western Pacific Region (WPRO), Americas (AMRO)

key_themes: Deep learning algorithm development and validation
key_lessons: Rigorous clinical validation essential for regulatory approval
Integration into existing workflows critical for adoption
Ethical frameworks needed for AI-assisted diagnosis
outcomes: First FDA-authorized autonomous AI diagnostic device
Expanded screening access in primary care settings

case_studies.title: DHIS2: Building Health Information Infrastructure Across 100+ Countries

geographic_focus.country: Global (originated in South Africa, scaled globally)

key_lessons: Open source reduces dependency and increases ownership
Flexibility enables adaptation to diverse contexts
Data use culture essential alongside technology
outcomes: Adoption as national HMIS in 100+ countries

examples: Pakistan Lady Health Worker digital programs

items.question: Which of the following best defines mHealth?
options: A. Electronic health records stored in cloud systems
B. Medical and public health practice supported by mobile devices
C. Telemedicine consultations via video conferencing
D. Artificial intelligence applications in diagnosis

items.question: Interoperability in health information systems refers to:
options: A. The ability of health workers to operate multiple devices
B. The ability of systems to exchange and use data coordinately
C. The operation of health facilities across international borders
D. The capacity of AI systems to perform multiple diagnostic tasks

items.question: Point-of-care testing is characterized by:
options: A. Testing performed in centralized reference laboratories
B. Testing requiring specialized laboratory technicians
C. Testing performed at or near the site of patient care
D. Testing that requires several days for results

items.question: The four main domains of health technology assessment include all EXCEPT:
options: A. Clinical effectiveness
B. Cost-effectiveness
C. Manufacturer profitability
D. Ethical considerations

items.question: Which organization provides guidance on health technology assessment methodologies globally?
options: A. World Trade Organization
B. International Network of Agencies for Health Technology Assessment
C. International Monetary Fund
D. World Bank Infrastructure Division

items.question: Frugal innovation in healthcare is best characterized by:
options: A. Developing technologies regardless of cost
B. Importing technologies from high-income countries
C. Minimizing resource use while meeting essential needs
D. Focusing exclusively on high-tech solutions

items.question: Deep learning, as applied in medical imaging AI, is best described as:
options: A. Rule-based programming for image analysis
B. Neural networks with multiple processing layers learning from data
C. Manual feature extraction by radiologists
D. Statistical analysis of imaging metadata

items.question: A major concern regarding AI algorithms trained predominantly on data from high-income country populations is:
options: A. The algorithms will be too accurate
B. Potential performance degradation in underrepresented populations
C. Increased computational efficiency
D. Reduced need for local validation studies

items.question: The Technology Acceptance Model identifies which factors as primary determinants of technology adoption?
options: A. Cost and availability
B. Perceived usefulness and perceived ease of use
C. Government mandate and regulatory approval
D. Manufacturer reputation and marketing

items.question: DHIS2 has become widely adopted as a national health information system because:
options: A. It is a proprietary system with extensive vendor support
B. It is open source, flexible, and supported by a global community
C. It is mandated by WHO for all member states
D. It requires minimal internet connectivity to function

items.question: Explain the concept of 'appropriate technology' in global health and provide two examples of how this principle has been applied in medical device design for low-resource settings.
model_answer_points: Definition: Technology suitable to social, economic, environmental conditions where used
Characteristics: affordable, maintainable, durable, meets local needs
Example 1: Portable ultrasound devices designed for rural settings without electricity
Example 2: Paper-based rapid diagnostic tests for infectious diseases
Importance of local context in design decisions

items.question: Describe three clinical applications of artificial intelligence in healthcare and discuss one key challenge associated with implementing AI diagnostics in low- and middle-income countries.
model_answer_points: Application 1: Medical imaging analysis (radiology, pathology, dermatology)
Application 2: Clinical decision support systems
Application 3: Predictive analytics for patient outcomes
Challenge: Algorithm bias from training data predominantly from HICs
Challenge: Infrastructure requirements (computing, connectivity)
Challenge: Regulatory frameworks and accountability

items.question: Using the RE-AIM framework, outline how you would evaluate a telemedicine program implemented in a rural district of a low-income country.
model_answer_points: Reach: Proportion of target population accessing telemedicine services
Effectiveness: Clinical outcomes, patient satisfaction, diagnostic accuracy
Adoption: Proportion of health facilities and providers using the system
Implementation: Fidelity to program design, adaptations made
Maintenance: Sustainability, institutionalization, long-term outcomes

items.question: Outline the key steps in conducting a health technology assessment for a new point-of-care diagnostic device intended for use in primary care settings in sub-Saharan Africa.
model_answer_points: Define scope and policy question
Assess clinical effectiveness through systematic review
Evaluate diagnostic accuracy in target population
Conduct cost-effectiveness analysis with local costs
Consider implementation feasibility and health system capacity
Address ethical issues including equity implications
Engage stakeholders including end users

items.question: Analyze three factors contributing to the digital divide in global health and propose strategies to address each factor.
model_answer_points: Factor 1: Infrastructure gaps - Strategy: investment in connectivity, offline solutions
Factor 2: Digital literacy - Strategy: training programs, user-centered design
Factor 3: Affordability - Strategy: subsidies, open source solutions
Additional: Gender disparities in technology access
Additional: Urban-rural divides

items.question: Discuss the ethical implications of using AI-powered diagnostic tools in healthcare settings, addressing issues of algorithmic bias, accountability, and patient autonomy.
model_answer_points: Algorithmic bias: Training data representation, performance disparities
Accountability: Liability when AI makes diagnostic errors
Patient autonomy: Informed consent for AI-assisted diagnosis
Transparency: Explainability of AI decision-making
Data privacy: Protection of sensitive health information
Equity: Ensuring AI benefits reach all populations

items.title: Implementation Planning Exercise
items.description: Using the Consolidated Framework for Implementation Research (CFIR), develop an implementation plan for introducing a mobile health application for community health workers in a district of your chosen country. Address intervention characteristics, outer setting, inner setting, individual characteristics, and implementation process.
expected_deliverables: Implementation strategy addressing each CFIR domain

items.title: Health Technology Assessment Exercise
items.description: Conduct a mini-HTA for a low-cost pulse oximeter intended for use in neonatal care units in low-resource settings. Include assessment of clinical need, effectiveness evidence, cost analysis, and implementation considerations.
expected_deliverables: Background on clinical need and target population
Cost analysis including acquisition and operational costs

items.title: Digital Divide Analysis
items.description: Select a low- or middle-income country and analyze the digital divide in health. Identify key barriers to equitable access to digital health technologies and propose a multi-sectoral strategy to address these barriers.

items.title: AI Ethics Case Analysis
items.description: Analyze a real-world case where an AI/ML algorithm used in healthcare demonstrated bias or produced disparate outcomes across population groups. Discuss the causes, consequences, and propose mitigation strategies.

items.title: Digital Health Intervention Design
items.description: Using human-centered design principles, design a digital health intervention to address a specific health challenge in a low-resource setting of your choice. The design should incorporate appropriate technology principles and address sustainability.
expected_deliverables: User research findings (hypothetical or based on literature)

items.title: Health Information System Strengthening Plan
items.description: Develop a comprehensive strategy for strengthening the health information system in a resource-limited country of your choice. Address data collection, management, analysis, and use for decision-making.
expected_deliverables: Gap analysis using WHO HIS framework

items.title: Technology Evaluation Exercise
items.description: Select an emerging health technology (e.g., AI diagnostic tool, digital therapeutic, wearable device) and conduct a comprehensive evaluation using an established HTA framework. Consider clinical effectiveness, cost-effectiveness, equity implications, and implementation feasibility.

items.title: Regulatory Framework Comparison
items.description: Compare and critically appraise the regulatory frameworks for AI-based medical devices in three different jurisdictions (e.g., US FDA, EU CE marking, and one LMIC regulatory authority). Analyze strengths, weaknesses, and implications for global health.
expected_deliverables: Critical appraisal of strengths and limitations

items.title: National Digital Health Strategy Development
items.scenario: You have been appointed as a consultant to advise the Ministry of Health in a lower-middle-income country on developing a national digital health strategy. The country has made progress in primary healthcare but faces challenges with fragmented health information systems, limited telemedicine capacity, and growing interest in AI-based diagnostics.
tasks: Conduct a situational analysis of the country's digital health landscape
Identify priority areas for digital health investment
Develop strategic objectives aligned with national health priorities
Propose governance structures for digital health
Address regulatory requirements for digital health and AI
Plan for digital health workforce development
Address equity considerations throughout the strategy
expected_deliverables: Strategic framework with vision, goals, and objectives

quality_indicators.evidence_level_distribution: Emphasis on systematic reviews and RCTs
